elements.sent-2411-001: Supported by the National Science Technology Council of Taiwan through Project NSTC 112-2115-M-005-003.
elements.sent-2411-002: This study investigates air pollution in central Taiwan, focusing on key pollutants, including SO$_2$, NO$_2$, PM$_{10}$, and PM$_{2.5}$.
elements.sent-2411-003: We use non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) to reduce data dimensionality, followed by wind direction analysis and speed to trace pollution sources.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our findings indicate that PM$_{2.5}$ and NO$_2$ levels are primarily influenced by local sources, while SO$_2$ levels are more affected by transboundary factors.
elements.sent-2411-005: For PM$_{10}$, contributions from domestic and transboundary sources are nearly equal.
elements.sent-2411-006: Keywords: Non-negative matrix factorization, air pollution, data visualization.
elements.sent-2411-007: Throughout history, the rapid advancement of human civilization and technology has led to significant environmental and air pollution challenges.
elements.sent-2411-008: The widespread use of transportation fuels and various energy sources has generated large quantities of pollutants.
elements.sent-2411-009: Major pollution events—such as the 1940 Los Angeles smog incident, the 1952 London Great Smog, the 1961 Yokkaichi asthma incident in Japan, and the 2013 air quality crisis in Mainland China—have been directly linked to high energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-010: These events have posed serious risks to human health, leading to various diseases, environmental damage, and a decline in the quality of life.
elements.sent-2411-011: The Taichung Thermal Power Plant (TTPP) in Taichung City has consistently drawn public and political attention, particularly during election cycles from 2009 to 2019.
elements.sent-2411-012: As Taipower’s largest thermal power plant, TTPP has a total installed capacity of 5.788 million kilowatts, representing approximately 20\% of Taiwan’s total power generation.
elements.sent-2411-013: From 2006 to February 2017, it was the world’s largest thermal power plant.
elements.sent-2411-014: This prominence highlights the importance of studying air pollution in central Taiwan, especially within the Greater Taichung City area.
elements.sent-2411-015: Public concern about air pollution is growing due to its harmful effects on health and the environment.
elements.sent-2411-016: Polluted air can damage the respiratory, cardiovascular, and nervous systems, with air pollutants taking various forms--solid particles, liquid droplets, and gases--each impacting health differently.
elements.sent-2411-017: A significant portion of atmospheric sulfur dioxide (SO$_2$) and nitrogen dioxide (NO$_2$) arises from fuel combustion in power plants, vehicles, and industries, although the contributions from each source vary.
elements.sent-2411-018: Both SO2 and NO2 can irritate the eyes, mouth, nose, and respiratory tract, and they are major contributors to acid rain.
elements.sent-2411-019: Particulate matter (PM) is categorized by particle size, primarily into PM$_{10}$, which includes particles 10 micrometers or smaller, and PM$_{2.5}$, which consists of finer particles 2.5 micrometers or smaller.
elements.sent-2411-020: SO$_2$ generally remains in the troposphere for about two days, or roughly one day in areas with high moisture at cloud tops.
elements.sent-2411-021: In central Taiwan, the mountain range disrupts prevailing winds, causing air pollution in inland cities and the southwest region largely from local sources.
elements.sent-2411-022: Kishcha et al. highlight that air pollution is directly associated with aerosol optical depth (AOD), which affects atmospheric water circulation, cloud formation, precipitation, and climate, demonstrating the broad indirect effects of pollution.
elements.sent-2411-023: In recent years, PM$_{2.5}$ has become a research focus due to its small size--smaller than bacteria--making it easily inhalable and potentially harmful to health.
elements.sent-2411-024: In 2015, PM$_{2.5}$ was identified as the leading contributor to the Global Burden of Disease \cite{long .}
elements.sent-2411-025: To address air pollution effectively, it is essential first to identify primary pollution sources and then develop practical, targeted solutions.
elements.sent-2411-026: In Taiwan, air pollution sources are categorized as either transboundary or domestic.
elements.sent-2411-027: Lin et al. \cite{wind note that transboundary pollution mainly originates from the Asian continent, especially in winter when the northeast monsoon prevails.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Key sources include dust storms, frontal pollution, and background air masses.
elements.sent-2411-029: According to Li et al. \cite{1-2 , PM$_{2.5}$ from transboundary pollution is largely sourced from North China and the East China Sea, with dust storms and northeast monsoons in spring and winter transporting Asian dust and artificial particles across the Taiwan Strait.}
elements.sent-2411-030: This paper examines the main sources of pollutants such as O$_3$, NO$_2$, PM$_{10}$, and PM$_{2.5}$ in central Taiwan.
elements.sent-2411-031: The paper is structured as follows: Section 2 outlines the methods, Section 3 describes data sources, Section 4 presents the analysis results, followed by a validation of the proposed method in Section 5.
elements.sent-2411-033: Non-negative matrix factorization (NMF), introduced by Lee and Seung in 1999 \cite{nmf3 , has become a valuable tool across various fields.}
elements.sent-2411-034: A key advantage of NMF is that all values in the resulting matrices are non-negative, which is especially beneficial in practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-035: In scientific research, matrix factorization is commonly used for efficient data processing, and NMF offers a simpler approach with easily interpretable results compared to traditional methods.
elements.sent-2411-036: Techniques such as PCA, SVD (Singular Value Decomposition), and QR factorization are often used for matrix factorization; however, they can produce negative values in the output even if all input elements are positive.
elements.sent-2411-037: Although these negative values may be mathematically valid, they are often irrelevant or impractical in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-038: For example, in image processing, where pixel values range from 0 to 255, negative values in the decomposition lack practical meaning.
elements.sent-2411-039: NMF effectively circumvents this issue by ensuring that all outputs are non-negative.
elements.sent-2411-040: Additionally, NMF has broad applications, including in speech processing, robotics, and fields such as biomedical and chemical engineering.
elements.sent-2411-041: We now introduce the NMF algorithm, following the approach outlined by Lee \cite{nmf1,nmf2 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: First, we represent the data as a matrix $A$ and apply the NMF method to decompose $A$ into two matrices, $W$ and $H$, as shown in the following equation: \begin{equation}A_{m \times n} \simeq W_{m \times k} \times H_{k \times n} = \hat{A}. \label{nmf}\end{equation} The non-negative initial matrices $W$ and $H$ are randomly initialized, and we use Euclidean distance to calculate the cost function, as shown in the following formula: \begin{equation}J(W,H)=\frac{1}{2}\Vert A-WH\Vert^2_{F}=\frac{1}{2}\sum_{ij}(A_{ij}-(WH)_{ij})^2. \label{cost}\end{equation} The multiplicative update rules are implemented to iterate until convergence, as shown in the following formula: \begin{equation}W_{il}=W_{il}\cdot \frac{(AH^T)_{il}}{(WHH^T)_{il}} , ~~H_{lj}=H_{lj}\cdot \frac{(W^TA)_{lj}}{(W^TWH)_{lj}}. \label{rules}\end{equation} The formula (\ref{rules ) ensures that the matrix elements are all positive values.}
elements.sent-2411-043: We will now prove the multiplication update algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-044: We derive it from matrix multiplication and differentiation: \begin{equation}(WH)_{ij}=\sum_{l}W_{il}H_{lj} ~~and~~ \frac{\partial(WH)_{ij}}{\partial W_{il}}=H_{lj}. \label{update}\end{equation} Then \begin{equation}\begin{split} \frac{\partial }{\partial W} J(W,H)\bigg|_{il} = \sum_{j}[-H_{lj}(A_{ij}-(WH)_{ij})]H_{lj} = \sum_{j} -A_{ij}H_{lj}+\sum_{j}(WH)_{ij}H_{lj} = -(AH^T)_{il}+(WHH^T)_{il}. \end{split} \label{partial}\end{equation} Following \eqref{partial , we have \begin{equation}\frac{\partial }{\partial H}J(W,H)\bigg|_{lj}=-(W^TA)_{lj}+(W^TWH)_{lj}. \label{partial1}\end{equation} Next, using the gradient, we have \begin{equation}W_{il}\Leftarrow W_{il}-\alpha_{1} \cdot [-(AH^T)_{il}+(WHH^T)_{il}],\end{equation} \begin{equation}H_{lj}\Leftarrow H_{lj}-\alpha_{2} \cdot [-(W^TA)_{lj}+(W^TWH)_{lj}].\end{equation}. Let \begin{equation}\alpha_{1}=\frac{W_{il}}{(WHH^T)_{il}}, \quad \alpha_{2}=\frac{H_{lj}}{(W^TWH)_{lj}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Then, we have the result of equation \eqref{rules .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Finally, one normalizes by converting the data to values between $0$ and $1$, and the formula is as follows: \begin{equation}H_{ij}= \begin{cases} \frac{H_{ij}-H_{\min}}{H_{\max}-H_{min}}, \mbox{if $H_{\min} < H_{\max}$} 1, \mbox{if $H_{\min} = H_{\max}$}. \end{cases} \label{nor}\end{equation} The NMF algorithm is described below.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{NMF} \label{alg:NMF} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE {Input: $A, k $, where $A$ is the data matrix, $k$ is the required dimension reduction.} \STATE {Step 1. Given an initial nonnegative matrices $W$ and $H$, normalize every row in matrix $H$.} \STATE {Step 2. While (not converged) for $i=1:m$, ~~~for $l=1:k$ \[ W_{il} \leftarrow W_{il}\cdot \frac{(AH^T)_{il}}{(WHH^T)_{il}} \] for $j=1:n$, ~~~for $l=1:k$ \[ H_{lj} \leftarrow H_{lj}\cdot \frac{(W^TA)_{lj}}{(W^TWH)_{lj}} \] Renormalize each row in $H$} End \STATE {Output: Matrices $W$ and $H$.} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-047: The Environmental Protection Department provided the research data over the years \cite{EPAA , consisting of hourly pollution concentration values.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Our study focuses on data collected from air quality monitoring stations across 14 regions in Taiwan, including Miaoli, Taichung, Changhua, and Nantou.
elements.sent-2411-049: The main pollutants analyzed are SO$_2$ (sulfur dioxide), NO$_2$ (nitrogen dioxide), PM$_{2.5}$, and PM$_{10}$.
elements.sent-2411-050: These pollutants are measured in parts per billion (ppb) for SO$_2$ and NO$_2$, and in $\mu g/m^3$ for PM$_{2.5}$ and PM$_{10}$.
elements.sent-2411-051: The study period spans from 2008 to 2017, encompassing 87,672 records.
elements.sent-2411-052: The data are stored in an 87,672 $\times$ 14 matrix for each pollutant, resulting in four matrices of the same dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-053: Fig. \ref{M illustrates the locations of the 14 monitoring stations.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Industrial air quality monitoring stations are positioned downwind of industrial areas to assess the impact of industrial pollution.
elements.sent-2411-055: General air quality monitoring stations are situated in densely populated regions where high pollution levels may occur, while the background air quality monitoring station is placed in areas with minimal synthetic pollution \cite{EPA .. \bgroup-.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \begin{center} \includegraphics[height=8cm]{map.jpg} \end{center} \caption{Central Taiwan Terrain Map: Stars indicate Industrial Air Quality Monitoring Stations; squares indicate Background Air Quality Monitoring Stations; and circles indicate General Air Quality Monitoring Stations.} \label{M} \end{center}

elements.sent-2411-058: Second, the stations with the highest concentrations of SO$_{2}$ are HH and CH in Changhua, with HH having the highest levels.
elements.sent-2411-059: The HH station is an industrial air quality monitoring station positioned downwind of the thermal power plant in Taichung and influenced by the prevailing northeast wind.
elements.sent-2411-060: Third, PM$_{10}$ concentrations rise after noon, especially at the EL, NT, CS, HT, and TL stations.
elements.sent-2411-061: Fourth, the stations with the lowest PM$_{2.5}$ concentrations are TF, ML, and SY, all located in Miaoli.
elements.sent-2411-062: The particulate matter levels in Miaoli are similar to those in other regions, but overall, the SY station has the best air quality in central Taiwan.
elements.sent-2411-063: In Fig. \ref{2.2 , we observe that the highest pollutant concentrations occur in March, while the lowest concentrations of NO$_{2}$, PM$_{10}$, and PM$_{2.5}$ are seen during the summer.}
elements.sent-2411-064: SO$_{2}$ concentration remains relatively consistent throughout the year.
elements.sent-2411-065: Additionally, seasonal data (Table \ref{bmonth ) show that pollution is more severe during the spring and winter.}

elements.sent-2411-069: The initial step involves identifying the optimal value of $k$, which yields $k$ features.
elements.sent-2411-070: Subsequently, by utilizing data on wind direction, wind speed, and pollution concentration, we ascertain whether each feature is linked to domestic or foreign sources of pollution.
elements.sent-2411-071: Prior to conducting the analysis, it is imperative to ascertain the appropriate value of k.
elements.sent-2411-072: For this purpose, we employ the method proposed by Jean et al. \cite{jean , wherein we select the value of k at which the Cophenetic Correlation begins to exhibit a decline.}
elements.sent-2411-073: According to Limem et al. \cite{Limem , the value of k must adhere to the stipulation $nk + km \ll nm$, which simplifies to $k \ll \min\{ n, m \}$.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Given that $k \ll 14$, we consider values within the range of $2 \leq k \leq 7$.. In Figs.

elements.sent-2411-143: Chen et al. \cite{D provide data on the proportion of air pollution in Taiwan for the year 2010.}
elements.sent-2411-144: They utilized data from coastal air monitoring stations along with specific meteorological conditions, including concentrations of transboundary pollution.
elements.sent-2411-145: They calculated the total concentration of pollutants by applying the Thiessen polygon area-weighting method.
elements.sent-2411-146: Consequently, the concentration of domestic pollution was derived by subtracting the concentration of transboundary pollution from the total concentration.
elements.sent-2411-147: Their findings indicate that the domestic pollution ratios for SO$_{2}$, NO$_{2}$, and O$_{3}$ are 27\%, 70\%, and 25\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-148: We will use these results to validate the findings of our NMF analysis and will explain the experimental results and methodology in the following sections.

elements.sent-2411-173: In this study, we employed the Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) method to diminish the dimensionality of air pollution data.
elements.sent-2411-174: Subsequently, we conducted an analysis of wind direction and speed to categorize each pollutant feature as either arising from domestic sources or transboundary origins.
elements.sent-2411-175: Table \ref{4 illustrates the percentages of contributions from domestic and transboundary sources for nitrogen dioxide (NO$_{2}$), sulfur dioxide (SO$_{2}$), particulate matter (PM$_{10}$), and particulate matter (PM$_{2.5}$).}
elements.sent-2411-176: The findings indicate that NO$_{2}$ and PM$_{2.5}$ pollution is predominantly influenced by sources within the domestic sphere, whereas transboundary sources significantly impact SO$_{2}$ pollution.
elements.sent-2411-177: For PM$_{10}$, the contributions from domestic and transboundary sources are nearly equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-178: Furthermore, we compared our results with those of Chen et al. \cite{D as presented in Table \ref{5 .}}
elements.sent-2411-179: Considering that our findings fall within a $\,\pm 6\%$ margin of deviation from those reported by Chen et al., we deduce that our methodologies are both effective and yield promising outcomes.
elements.figure-2411-008: [H] \begin{center} \caption{Proportion of domestic and transboundary pollution.} \label{4} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|} proportion transboundary pollution ratio domestic pollution ratio NO$_{2}$ 33.64\% 66.36\% SO$_{2}$ 54.17\% 45.83\% PM$_{10}$ 49.05\% 50.95\% PM$_{2.5}$ 35.35\% 64.65\% \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-009: [H] \begin{center} \caption{Method validation for 2010 data.} \label{5} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|} contaminant Chen \cite{D} NMF NO$_{2}$ 70\% 75.9\% SO$_{2}$ 27\% 26.9\% O$_{3}$ 25\% 22.7\% \end{tabular} \end{center}
